sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=40, col=33), MOUSE(row=59, col=27).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 41, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking object center moves white diamond there and shifts clicked object along path toward next object. Oscillating between magenta center [56,27] and dark gray diamond [60,27] is the current mechanic.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'purple_ring': 'hollow diamond ring at ~21,39', 'white_diamond': 'white diamond that follows clicks, moves to clicked position', 'gray_path': 'reconfigurable diagonal path', 'purple_square': 'purple square with magenta center at [56,27]', 'dark_gray_diamond': 'dark gray diamond at [60,27]'}
- Win pattern: HYPOTHESIS: Continue oscillating clicks until level completes
Current level:
- World model: Oscillating chain: click magenta at [56,27] → click dark gray at [60,27], repeat. White diamond alternates between these two positions.
- Goal model: Continue chain until level completes
- Recent findings: ['Batched 4 clicks (2 cycles) successfully at steps 10-13', 'Pattern oscillates between magenta [56,27] and dark gray [60,27]']
- Open questions: ['When does oscillation stop? How many total cycles needed?']
- Plan: Batch oscillate between magenta [56,27] and dark gray [60,27] in groups of 4-8 clicks
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 40 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, I can see:
1. The purple ring is still at the top
2. The purple square with magenta center is in the middle
3. The dark gray diamond has moved up - it's now near the purple square
4. The white diamond is at the bottom (where the dark gray diamond used to be)
5. There's a visible white path connecting them
It seems like clicking objects moves the white diamond there AND moves the clicked object along the path. When I clicked the purple square, it moved the dark gray diamond up. When I clicked the dark gray diamond, the white diamond moved there.
The pattern seems to be: clicking an object moves it along the path toward the next object. The white diamond follows clicks.
Looking at the current state:
- Purple ring at top (unchanged)
- Purple square in middle
- Dark gray diamond moved up from bottom to near purple square
- White diamond at bottom
I need to figure out what completes the level. Maybe I need to move all objects to the ring? Or maybe I need to click in a specific order. Let me try clicking the dark gray diamond again (now at its new position), then the purple square, then the ring.
Let me first check where the dark gray diamond is now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find key objects by color and size
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        print(f"Dark gray diamond: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    elif n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 20:
        print(f"Purple square: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    elif n['color'] == 'M':
        print(f"Magenta: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    elif n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        print(f"White diamond: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Dark gray diamond center
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = [(min(rows)+max(rows))//2, (min(cols)+max(cols))//2]
        print(f"Dark gray center: {center}")
        # Click dark gray, then purple square, then ring
        action([
            {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center[0], 'col': center[1]},
            {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 33},
            {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 39},
        ])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking moves white diamond to clicked position and shifts clicked object along path"}, "object_taxonomy": {"purple_ring": "hollow diamond ring at ~21,39", "white_diamond": "white diamond that follows clicks", "purple_square": "purple square with magenta center", "dark_gray_diamond": "dark gray diamond that moves along path"}, "hud_map": {}, "win_pattern": "HYPOTHESIS: Move all objects to ring by clicking in sequence", "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Objects on path: ring (top), purple square (middle), dark gray diamond (moved up). White diamond at bottom.", "goal_model": "Click objects in sequence to move them along path to ring", "recent_findings": ["Clicking purple square moved dark gray diamond up", "Clicking dark gray diamond moved white diamond there"], "open_questions": ["What sequence completes the level?"], "current_plan": "Click dark gray, purple square, ring in sequence", "strategy_log": ["Oscillation was wrong", "Single sequence click may work"], "failed_probes": ["Oscillating clicks"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
